text: At Teton A.T. we love to ski, at Teton A.T. the mountains and me, at Teton A.T. we ski to be free, we love to ski at Teton A.T.
First thing you want to do when you're going to wax your climbing skins, get the iron all warmed up, nice low temp, 60 degrees C, 140 degrees Fahrenheit is a good place to start.
You don't want to have to crank the iron up too much to melt the wax, there's always a danger of burning your skins, the hairs will kind of curl up and bite into the snow even more.
Good for grip, not that good for glide.
Keep it low, experiment with the different types of waxes you're using, just kind of see what works best with you.
These are skins before being waxed, the pelt's not super smooth, hairs are sort of fuzzy, not that buffed out.
Once your rig's all lined up in the vise, just start.
Rub a good dose of wax on there, you don't want to get too gloppy, but you can go over it once and then hit some spots that didn't get it too hard.
Obviously you don't want to get it gooed up too much, this is about the max you really want it to look like.
The more you put on there, the more you're going to have to heat up the iron and get it all hot, danger of burning your skins, so don't go too heavy.
Once you've got it all waxed up, bust out the iron, get it all going and it's nice to just do nice evens.
You can even pass, tip to tail, obviously you'd want to go back and forth.
But you can kind of feel it melt and see it melting into the skin, it's kind of a cool deal to watch the hairs get all in alignment.
It's a little bit harder to get the edges for some reason, so usually I go back down and focus on the edges a little bit more.
Try to get them nice and melted in there.
And you can kind of see sections where the wax hasn't really melted.
So you'll kind of live and learn with this part.
This is my backcountry rig, so I'm going to go over and give her another dose because I probably won't want to do this again for a little bit.
But you know your rando setup, you might want to be a little bit more delicate.
You can use fluorinated waxes, maybe to save them for race day, really wet conditions.
You can't really see it helping out in colder temps, but I've experimented with some colder temperature waxes, ironing them in.
You know, it just gets sketchy with the iron burning your bases, so you know, a yellow or a pink, something like that.
As you can see here, this is after waxing, all the pelt is really lined up, it's really uniform, nice and flat, gives you really good glide, resistance to glopping up.
I've also experimented with brushing them with a wire brush, that seems to really help, maybe in between ironing.
So anyway, happy skiing, thanks a lot, live to ski!
